speaker: interviewer
text: --with Colonel Warren Dunn. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You told us that before you retired, you experienced another human tragedy. Would you like to tell us about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was associated with-- with a program that I was assigned to. I--I was consulting on a program called Galileo, which was to launch a spacecraft to Jupiter, send in a probe into the bowels of Jupiter, and then orbit for two years. And so Marge and I went to Florida with the spacecraft to get it ready to launch on this program to go to Jupiter. And we had seen a beautiful launch of the Columbia, shuttle Columbia, early in the morning. And it was a gorgeous launch. And two weeks later, they were about to launch Challenger. And so we went early in the morning. Happened to be very cold that day. As a matter of fact, we went by-- we were living in Cape Canaveral in a condominium. We went by a place that, apparently, the sprinklers had come on in the early morning, and they-- everything was frozen. All the bushes had ice on them. It was very, very cold. And so we went out, and we were sitting in the stands across from the launch area for the shuttle Columbia-- shuttle Challenger. And it was launched. And you know, I'm sure everybody knows what happened to Challenger. It was nine miles up and blew up. And a terrible, terrible tragedy for the space program and for everybody else in the world, I think. Seven young astronauts were killed. It was-- I'm not sure that I related it to some of the other death experiences I'd seen, but maybe so. The fact that I knew two of the people on it, the commander and the pilot of-- of that Challenger, made it even worse. And of course, the school teacher who was killed and the other astronauts, it was extremely tragic to me, personally. I could not-- I could not look at the ocean. I could not look at the sky. I could not look at clouds, because that's what I saw when the Challenger blew up. I remember Marge asking me when she saw the parachutes, oh, they got out. And I said, no, honey. They didn't. Those parachutes were there for the purpose of lowering the-- the big, solid booster shells that were depleted by then down in the ocean and they're retrieved. So no, they didn't survive it. And more unfortunately was the fact that they lived all the way down to the ocean, which was even more traumatic to find that out, that they lived for all the time that it took to go from nine miles up, to die when the shuttle capsule hit the ocean. And they know that was the case, because the bodies were recovered and-- and the evidence is that they did survive. But it was so traumatic, almost more traumatic than some of the experiences in combat. Unexpected, I guess, was one of the reasons. The fact that I was so intimately involved in a program that-- that had so much merit. And it-- it killed our program. It killed every other space program for a long time. And it killed seven really talented, good people. And that was-- it was a tragedy that I had a long time getting over. As I say, even more so, maybe, than the deaths I experienced in combat. And I'm not sure why that was. Maybe it was a re-acquaintance with what I went through in my younger years when I was in combat. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have some kind of a message for humanity? What-- what would you-- what kind of legacy would you like to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, don't let it ever happen again, what happened in Nazi Germany. And I think the more knowledge we gain about what happened then, the better we're going to be in preventing it from ever happening again, because it shouldn't ever have happened. We've got to be on-- tremendously on the alert to not let the skinheads and the-- and the neo-Nazis, and those people that want to lead us into the path of destruction again, never let that happen again. The other is to do everything that you think you have the ability to do, and don't shy away from it. I--I feel that so strongly. Marge and I both do, that if you want to do something bad enough, you can do it. Whether you are extremely talented in the direction that you want to go or not, you can do it. That would be-- those would be my-- the two things that stand uppermost in my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you say that to your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I do. All the time. All the time. And Marge says it too. We both are of the same mind in that direction, that if we want to do something bad enough, we'll do it. And that's what we do.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we will let you introduce Marge to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Colonel, would you introduce your wife to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Marjorie, Marjorie Temple, my lovely wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us a little bit about her.

speaker: interviewee
text: She is a former consultant-- well, I guess she still consults. We've been married now almost 20 years, 20 years next February. We met back in the '60s.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a matter of fact, she and I worked together at Aerojet General that I talked about earlier. And in our marriage, I mentioned my children. She has three that have joined us. And they--I feel like they're my kids too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have lived in Monterey since our marriage and enjoy this area so very much. And she was, at one time, president of an electronics company here in Monterey. And I think I mentioned that to you. And also a consultant after her work experience there. And as I said before, we formed a consulting firm. And she in the-- the business organization development management area, and I in the rocket propulsion.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Dunn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you married him, did you know he was a hero?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was my hero. Yeah, I think I knew. He had--he had shared with me, I believe, quite a bit that he hadn't shared before. I think he was the one that didn't know he was a hero. I think that's the way it worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you convince him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I have. I've tried to. I've tried to-- I tried to make it all a part of his daily life. All of his medals were packed away, his-- his trail map was packed away. That whole part of his life was packed away. And so I unpacked it and put it on the walls and said, this is your life. And it's part of your life to live with. And in doing that, I think he was able to feel more comfortable with some of the pain that was there. And the other piece for me was I had always felt, after I knew him, that he needed to go back and see it healthy. And he didn't think he needed it. He just kept saying, no, I'm all right. I was there, and I'm all right. Well, I--I didn't believe that. And I think I was the force behind the trip going back. I just kept saying, I think you need to go back. And we did attend a reunion. And he talked to some men that had gone back. And that maybe arose some interest for him, I think. But we went back-- my purpose for going back was for him to relive it five years ago, six years ago, not to necessarily relive it back in the '40s, but he had to go back and face that before he could see what it was today. And it was a healing experience, but it was a painful experience.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you both went to Dachau.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, we both retraced his entire combat trail. And then we went to Dachau together. And I-- I'm not sure what he's told you about Dachau, but it-- all that that he had blocked for so long came up. And we--but we had to deal with it together so--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it mean to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What it meant to me to be there with him was for me to experience a piece of his life that nobody could understand unless they were there. I know I can't understand what it was when he walked through those gates, but in his experience going back, and relating all those things that he had forgotten or he'd thought he had forgotten, it really truly did make me admire him as a hero. And also, it--it-- it made me urge him to share that with his children. He had not shared with his children that part of his life. And I thought it was important that that happen. And for me, it was a chance for him to bring to his children a piece of their life that they didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we thank you both.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Will you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my mother. Her name was Leone Waite Creps. And her mother, my grandmother, her name is-- was Nina Creps. And I knew her better as Nina [? Condit, ?] because that's who she was married to in the latter part of her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken, I believe, in about 1907.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Seattle, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my dad when he was-- went first into the military. And it was-- he was 17 then. And it was taken in Vancouver Barracks, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clarence Albert Dunn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Yes. This is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is my mother. I believe she was probably 18 at the time. And that would have been in-- since she was born in 1902, so that would be, like, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I believe that was taken also in Seattle, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leone [? Waite ?] [? Creps. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father and mother in their marriage-- wedding photograph.

speaker: interviewer
text: Names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their names is-- my mother is Leone Waite Creps. And my dad, Clarence Albert Dunn. It would have been taken in 1923. And that would have been in Vancouver, Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. This picture is?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a photograph of my dad when he was a major. His name was, at that time, Major Clarence Albert Dunn. And that was taken in-- I believe 1942. And that would have been in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about this picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my sister and I. My sister on the left, whose name was then Betty Nina Rebecca Dunn. It's presently Betty Cosgrove. And I'm on the right. And my name is Warren Courtland Dunn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- the photograph was taken in Pullman, Washington in 1928.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Second Lieutenant Warren C. Dunn. It was taken in Camp Gruber, Oklahoma, in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Before--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to Europe.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went into combat in Europe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell us about this map.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a map of the combat trail that the 42nd Infantry Rainbow Division traversed, starting in Marseille, France, and ending in Southern Austria, not too far from Innsbruck, Austria, in a little-- in my case, in a little town named [? Schwarz. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was on this trail that you came across the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. You can see it-- over on the right-hand side of the map is a depiction of where Dachau was with respect to Munich, for instance, and south of Nuremberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photograph is from a combat history of the 42nd Division, and it shows a photograph of-- of one of the battalion commanders, Colonel [? Don Donnard ?], and the tank destroyer captain lifting a survivor from a train load of bodies. He apparently survived in this 40-car train load of hundreds of bodies by being under some of them. One of our sergeants heard the moan-- moaning come. And he called to Colonel [? Donnard ?] and the captain that he believes there's somebody alive there. And he was. And they got him out of the boxcar. And he apparently survived. And he was luckier than some of the internees at Dachau, because several of them, when we, the liberators, came were so-- so elated that they ran toward us and killed themselves on electrified fence. The power had not been shut off. And that was one of the things that I had forgotten. And when we went six years ago back to Dachau, I saw the insulators on the fence and recalled that, and felt like, oh-- oh, boy, I'm a liberator and an executioner at the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Please describe.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture you're seeing here is of that survivor that Colonel [? Donnard ?] and the-- and the tank destroyer captain saved from the boxcar. And he's being taken to an ambulance to take him to treat him. And as I said before, he apparently survived, because we heard from Colonel [? Donnard ?] that he had indeed survived and lived a ripe old age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us again where and when was this.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, again, at Dachau, just outside the camp. These 40 boxcars were sitting on the rail head just outside the camp. And that's the first thing we saw when we got to the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the date when this happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was April 29, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. What do we see here?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of several of the internees that had been killed in the gas chamber. And these bodies were stacked up in the crematorium awaiting cremation, but they never were because they'd run out of fuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Dachau. And this picture would have been in-- again, in April of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a shadow box that my wife, Marge, put together for me. She found medals and insignia dating back to my ROTC days and put them together. And when she showed that to me, I cried. [CHUCKLES] In the center of the shadow box is a photograph of a young lieutenant, Lieutenant Warren Dunn, First Lieutenant Warren Dunn, taken south of Munich, Germany in May of 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this shows the progression of rank from second lieutenant and first lieutenant to captain. I became a captain in the reserve, then to major, to lieutenant colonel, and finally to full colonel, also known as bird colonel. I happened to be promoted to bird colonel when I was assistant commandant of an Army Reserve school in Fort MacArthur in San Pedro, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to tell us about the napkin ring.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, let's go up to that. This is a napkin ring made out of wood. And it has my name and the-- the rainbow colors. And this was made for a huge banquet at a school that we called Rainbow University in Zell am See, Austria. It was-- they were ordered to be made by our division commander for this big banquet. Happened to-- the chef for this banquet happened to be a former Hitler chef, but he had been repatriated and given up the thought of Hitler. But that was on our dinner tables. And Marge found it in a-- in a foot locker. And that's pretty-- pretty much of a treasure, for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you [? much. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is-- are two medals. The one on the left is a Silver Star. And that was awarded when I-- I described earlier in this conversation. It was awarded for gallantry in action. I had gotten tired of sitting there getting shot at. And so I got up and hailed my troops, and we ran down and captured a dozen or so Germans. And apparently, someone thought that that was worth a citation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the napkin ring that I was talking about earlier. And it's quite significant in that it was a-- really a presentation from our general to thank us for the fact that we had set up a successful school for Army personnel in our sector of the Army of occupation. And it was-- we took over a hotel and called in instructors and teachers to teach people. And many people were able to get their high school diplomas and pre-college education before they went back home. And I, being the Information and Education Officer for the 42nd Division, starting in October of '45, I was responsible for overseeing the conduct of education in that school.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can you imagine? 21.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph taken at the Jet Propulsion Lab in Pasadena, California. It's a mockup of the propulsion system that was built for the Mariner Mars orbiting spacecraft that photographed 80% of Mars. And the men the picture-- I am on the left, Warren C. Dunn. And I was the Operations Manager for the propulsion system. The man on the right is one of my group personnel engineer. His name was Theodore [? Metz. ?] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a photograph taken at Cape Canaveral. You can see in the background is the Atlas-Centaur that launched the Mariner Mars 71 spacecraft that went to Mars and orbited, took photographs of the surface of Mars. The people in the-- in the photograph are my engineers and technicians. I was the operations manager for the-- that program, where Dave Quarles one of the technicians. One of the other engineers was Jack Patzold. He's in the blue shirt. Bob Vincent, who was the quality assurance individual. Next, in the yellow shirt, is myself, Warren Dunn, and Jim Lumsden, another technician at the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph taken at my daughter Shelly's wedding. Standing are my son, Jimmy Dunn. Richard Hardwick is next, the tallest, and myself, Warren Dunn. Seated are my daughter, Claudia, who is wife of the tall fellow, Dick Hardwick. Claudia Hardwick--

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left side?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side. And in the middle is Shelly on her wedding day in her wedding dress, and then my wife, Marjorie, in the burgundy dress on the right, Marjorie Dunn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Standing on the right is you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: On the other side is your son.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my son-in-law in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two years ago.

speaker: interviewee
text: '94. Yes. In-- near Leesburg, Virginia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
